Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
South Asian Traditions

Nāgārjuna

c. 150 – 250 CE

A radical critique of intrinsic nature that destabilizes any reified account of mind, self, or world.

Traditional depictions of Nāgārjuna are devotional and symbolic, not historical portraits. Historically, very little can be stated securely about Nāgārjuna’s life beyond the broad scholarly consensus that Nāgārjuna was a Buddhist monk, probably active between roughly 150 and 250 CE, likely in southern India; even the textual corpus is complicated by later works being attributed to the famous name “Nāgārjuna.”

Nāgārjuna and the philosophy of mind: a rigorous reconstruction

Nāgārjuna did not write a “philosophy of mind” in the modern disciplinary sense. His central works, especially the Mūlamadhyamakakārikā or Fundamental Verses on the Middle Way, are not organized around consciousness, qualia, intentionality, neural reduction, or personal identity as contemporary analytic philosophy would frame them. But Nāgārjuna’s critique of intrinsic nature, his account of dependent origination, and his analysis of self, perception, language, causality, truth, and conceptual construction together yield one of the most radical philosophies of mind in world philosophy.

The core thesis is this: mind is not a substance, consciousness is not an ultimate ground, the subject-object structure is not metaphysically primitive, and the self is neither identical with nor separate from the psycho-physical processes conventionally taken to be “the person.” Mental life is real enough for practical, ethical, and soteriological purposes, but it is empty of any self-sufficient essence. Nāgārjuna’s philosophy of mind is therefore neither dualist, physicalist, idealist, eliminativist, nor simple phenomenalist. It is a relational, anti-foundational, anti-essentialist, therapeutic account of cognitive-affective life.

1. Historical position: where Nāgārjuna stands

Nāgārjuna is the founding or paradigmatic philosopher of Madhyamaka, the “Middle Way” school of Mahāyāna Buddhism. The Madhyamaka school became one of the two principal philosophical schools of Indian Mahāyāna Buddhism, the other being Yogācāra. Madhyamaka is called “middle” because it seeks to avoid the opposed metaphysical extremes of eternalism, the view that things exist through fixed essences, and annihilationism, the view that things with essences are later destroyed. Madhyamaka avoids both by denying that anything has an inherent nature, or svabhāva, in the first place.

The principal text is the Mūlamadhyamakakārikā (MMK), a work of about 450 stanzas that later Buddhist philosophers treated as the central text of the Middle Way. It examines causation, motion, perception, aggregates, agency, self, time, bondage, liberation, the four noble truths, and nirvāṇa. Nāgārjuna’s influence extended through Indian philosophical debates for roughly a millennium and, through the spread of Buddhism, became foundational in Tibetan, Chinese, Japanese, and other Asian traditions.

In the internal history of Buddhist philosophy of mind, Nāgārjuna inherits the early Buddhist denial of a permanent self and the analysis of persons into five aggregates: material form, sensation or feeling, apperception/perception, dispositional formations or volitions, and consciousness. Buddhist theories of mind generally reject the idea that mental phenomena require a substantial, independent, enduring self; instead, persons are analyzed as psycho-physical constituents and processes.

Nāgārjuna also stands after the development of Abhidharma, the Buddhist scholastic project that systematized experience into momentary dharmas, or psycho-physical events. Abhidharma sharpened Buddhist psychology but also tended, from a Madhyamaka perspective, to risk reifying the very elements it analyzed. The Abhidharma doctrine of momentariness treated dharmas as psycho-physical events arising and ceasing in consciousness, and the notion of intrinsic nature, svabhāva, became important in Abhidharma attempts to classify and ontologize dharmas.

Nāgārjuna’s position is therefore best understood as a second-order critique of Buddhist and non-Buddhist metaphysics alike. Against Brahmanical schools that posited a stable self, substance, or ultimate ground, Nāgārjuna argues that such entities cannot be coherently established. Against Buddhist reductionists who replace the self with ultimately real momentary dharmas, Nāgārjuna argues that the aggregates, mental events, and consciousness are empty too. He radicalizes the no-self doctrine: there is no substantial self, but there is also no substantial non-self basis out of which the self is built.

This matters for philosophy of mind because Nāgārjuna does not merely deny the soul. Many Western anti-dualists also deny the soul. Nāgārjuna goes further: he denies that mind, consciousness, perception, mental events, causal relations, bodily constituents, linguistic meanings, or epistemic instruments can function as intrinsic foundations. His philosophy of mind is not a reduction from self to mind, or from mind to matter, but a critique of the very need for an ultimate substrate.

2. The fundamental concept: emptiness of intrinsic nature

The central concept in Nāgārjuna is śūnyatā, emptiness. Emptiness does not mean non-existence. It means emptiness of svabhāva: intrinsic nature, inherent existence, independent essence, self-grounding being. The Stanford Encyclopedia’s Nāgārjuna entry distinguishes an ontological dimension of svabhāva, concerning how things exist, and a cognitive dimension, concerning how human beings conceptualize things. This second dimension is crucial for philosophy of mind: the error is not merely a bad theory but a deep cognitive habit of superimposing fixed essence onto fluid, dependent phenomena.

Nāgārjuna’s target is not ordinary functionality. He is not saying that tables, bodies, thoughts, pains, memories, or persons are nothing at all. He is saying that none of them exists from its own side, independently of causes, conditions, parts, conceptual practices, and relational contexts. The MMK famously states that whatever is dependently arisen is explained as emptiness; that, being dependently designated, is the middle path.

This is the pivot of a Nāgārjunian philosophy of mind. A pain is not unreal; a perception is not unreal; a person is not unreal. But none is self-grounding. Pain depends on body, nervous system, attention, aversion, memory, language, practical context, and self-relation. Perception depends on sensory organs, objects, consciousness, conceptualization, and practical discrimination. The person depends on aggregates, social designation, memory, action, and ethical continuity. To say these are empty is to say that their reality is relational, not intrinsic.

Nāgārjuna also refuses to make emptiness itself into a new metaphysical absolute. On Madhyamaka’s radical interpretation of the two truths, even ultimate truth is empty of intrinsic nature; emptiness is not a hidden substrate beneath appearances.

That point is indispensable. Many philosophies of mind try to solve the mind problem by identifying the real ground: soul, matter, brain, consciousness, representation, computation, pure awareness, language, social practice, or embodiment. Nāgārjuna’s method is to show that any such ground, if treated as intrinsically real, collapses under analysis. The correct “ground” is groundlessness: dependent origination without an ultimate self-subsisting base.

3. Two truths: why mind is conventionally real but ultimately empty

Nāgārjuna’s doctrine of two truths distinguishes conventional truth, saṃvṛti-satya, from ultimate truth, paramārtha-satya. Conventional truth concerns the world of ordinary discourse, practical interaction, causation, ethics, persons, sensations, and cognition. Ultimate truth concerns the emptiness of all phenomena. But the two are not two separate worlds. The ultimate is the emptiness of the conventional; the conventional is the only domain in which emptiness can be taught, understood, and practiced.

In MMK 24, Nāgārjuna says that the teaching of the Buddhas relies on two truths and that without relying on conventions, the ultimate meaning cannot be taught; without understanding the ultimate, nirvāṇa cannot be attained.

For philosophy of mind, this gives a powerful framework. At the conventional level, there are persons, intentions, perceptions, pains, beliefs, memories, attention, agency, and moral responsibility. These are not to be eliminated from ordinary explanation. At the ultimate level, none of these has intrinsic nature. The task is not to deny mental life but to stop mistaking its useful conventional structures for metaphysical primitives.

This yields a “two-level” philosophy of mind:

At the conventional level, mental processes can be studied, described, therapeutically transformed, and ethically evaluated. At the ultimate level, no mental process, subject, object, experience, or self has essence. The two truths prevent both nihilism and reification. They also prevent a common modern mistake: assuming that because the self is constructed, it is unreal in every sense. Nāgārjuna’s answer is subtler: the self is conventionally operative but ultimately empty.

4. The self: neither soul nor mere reduction base

Nāgārjuna’s most direct contribution to philosophy of mind concerns selfhood. In MMK 18, he argues that if the self were identical with the aggregates, it would arise and perish as they do; if it were different from the aggregates, it would not have their characteristics.

This is not merely a denial of an immortal soul. It is a critique of the entire logic by which a self is posited. The self cannot be identical with the body, feelings, perceptions, dispositions, and consciousness, because those are multiple, changing, and causally conditioned. But the self also cannot be wholly other than them, because then it would be disconnected from experience, agency, memory, and suffering. If the self is neither identical with nor different from the aggregates, then the self is dependently designated upon them.

The Stanford entry summarizes Nāgārjuna’s alternative: the self is not a cognitive nucleus that remains constant amid changing impressions and deliberations; it is the interconnected set of sensory and mental events, a sequence of physical and psychological processes standing in temporal and causal relations. Importantly, Madhyamaka does not then treat the aggregates as ultimately real building blocks. The self depends on the aggregates, but the aggregates are themselves empty and acquire their roles within the broader stream conventionally designated as a person.

This position differs from simple bundle theory. A bundle theory might say: there is no self, only mental events. Nāgārjuna’s view is more radical: there is no self with intrinsic nature, but there are also no mental events with intrinsic nature. The “bundle” is not an ultimate replacement for the person. The aggregates are not metaphysical atoms. The person is neither a substance nor a fiction in the sense of being useless or false. The person is a dependent designation with causal, ethical, and practical efficacy.

In modern terms, this means Nāgārjuna rejects all of the following as ultimate accounts of personal identity: Cartesian ego, soul-substance, bare subject, transcendental owner, biological organism as intrinsically self-identical, brain-self, psychological continuity as an essence, narrative identity as a final ground, and eliminativist denial of persons. Each may have conventional use, but none has svabhāva.

5. Consciousness: not a foundation, not a substance, not a final witness

A Nāgārjunian philosophy of mind cannot make consciousness ultimate. This sharply distinguishes Madhyamaka from many Indian and Western philosophies that seek a secure base in awareness.

In MMK 3, Nāgārjuna analyzes seeing. He lists the six sense faculties, including mind, and says that seeing does not see itself. He also says that consciousness arises in dependence on an eye and a visual form, just as a child arises in dependence on father and mother.

The point is not a crude denial of experience. The point is that consciousness is dependently arisen. Seeing is not self-established; the seer is not self-established; the seen is not self-established. None can be isolated as the intrinsic ground of the perceptual episode. Seeing is intelligible only within a triadic or networked relation among faculty, object, consciousness, and the broader conceptual-practical context.

This is a direct challenge to “witness consciousness” theories, Cartesian introspection, and any doctrine that treats awareness as self-luminous in a metaphysically foundational sense. Later Buddhist epistemologists such as Dignāga and Dharmakīrti develop sophisticated accounts of reflexive awareness, and Yogācāra philosophers argue that what we perceive is the appearance of an object within cognition itself.

Nāgārjuna’s Madhyamaka can accommodate the conventional fact that awareness occurs, but it resists turning awareness into an ultimate, self-certifying metaphysical base. Consciousness is not nothing, but it is not sovereign. It is not the owner of experience, not the container of the world, not the final witness behind appearances. It is another dependently arisen phenomenon.

6. Perception: subject and object are co-dependent

For Nāgārjuna, perception is not a relation between two intrinsically formed poles: an already complete subject and an already complete object. Rather, the seer, seeing, and seen are mutually dependent designations. In MMK 3, he argues that without a seer, there can be no stable account of what-is-seen and seeing; yet the seer itself is not available apart from seeing.

This has major implications for intentionality, the “aboutness” of mind. In much Western philosophy after Brentano, intentionality is treated as a defining mark of the mental: mental states are about objects. A Nāgārjunian account would not deny that experiences are object-directed. But it would deny that act, object, and directedness possess intrinsic natures. The intentional relation is itself empty.

That means there is no pure, self-contained “mental act” that then reaches out to an independently constituted object. Nor is there a purely external object that imposes itself on a passive mind. Perception is a dependent event: sensory faculty, object, consciousness, attention, affect, habit, language, memory, and practical orientation arise together in a structured but non-foundational way.

This also means that perceptual illusion is not a special case proving that experience is “inside the mind.” Illusion shows that perceptual objects are not self-certifying, but ordinary successful perception is also dependently structured. Madhyamaka does not infer from illusion to idealism. It instead generalizes dependence: veridical and non-veridical perception alike lack intrinsic nature.

7. Concepts, language, and mental proliferation

Nāgārjuna’s philosophy of mind is also a philosophy of conceptualization. The mind does not merely register the world; it fabricates, differentiates, imputes, and proliferates. In Buddhist psychology, mental proliferation, prapañca, is the process by which perception is elaborated into conceptual fixation, self-reference, craving, aversion, and metaphysical reification.

The Buddhist canonical model already links sensory contact, feeling, perception, thinking, and mental proliferation: dependent on eye and forms, visual consciousness arises; contact conditions feeling; feeling conditions perception; perception conditions thinking; thinking conditions proliferation.

Nāgārjuna radicalizes this. In MMK 18, he says that action and affliction come from thoughts, thoughts from fixations, and fixations are stopped by emptiness.

This is the psychological heart of Madhyamaka. Suffering does not arise merely because the mind has representations. It arises because the mind reifies its representations. It takes what is dependent, shifting, perspectival, and conventionally designated to be intrinsically real. The “I” becomes an owner; pain becomes “my pain”; perception becomes “the object as it really is”; belief becomes “the truth from its own side”; identity becomes a solid essence.

Nāgārjuna’s critique of language supports this. The Madhyamaka view is incompatible with a “ready-made world” whose intrinsic structure language simply mirrors. If nothing has svabhāva, nothing bears an intrinsic structure waiting to be copied by sentences. Truth cannot be a simple correspondence between intrinsically structured language and an intrinsically structured world.

For philosophy of mind, this implies that mental content is not a private item with an intrinsic meaning. Content is dependent on practices of discrimination, use, embodiment, causality, social convention, and pragmatic success. Meaning is not locked inside the head; nor is it simply stamped into the mind by the world. It is dependently designated.

8. Epistemology: no self-established means of knowing

Nāgārjuna’s philosophy of mind includes a critique of epistemic self-foundation. Against Nyāya-style realism, which sought reliable means of knowledge, pramāṇas, that disclose mind-independent objects, Nāgārjuna argues that epistemic instruments and epistemic objects cannot be intrinsically established. A means of knowledge is identified as such only in relation to the object known, and the object known is identified through the functioning of means of knowledge. They are mutually established.

This is a decisive anti-Cartesian move before Descartes. There is no pure foundation in immediate self-certifying cognition. Perception, inference, testimony, introspection, and conceptual analysis are all conventionally usable, but none is intrinsically authoritative. Their authority is contextual, relational, corrigible, and practice-dependent.

For philosophy of mind, this undermines several temptations:

First, introspection is not an infallible revelation of mental essence. Second, perception is not a self-validating window onto intrinsic reality. Third, inference is not a context-free machine that establishes metaphysical foundations. Fourth, consciousness does not certify itself as ultimate merely by occurring. Fifth, the distinction between appearance and reality cannot be grounded by appeal to a non-empty epistemic instrument.

Nāgārjuna’s epistemology is not skepticism in the simple sense that knowledge is impossible. Rather, it is anti-foundational contextualism. We know, act, correct, infer, teach, and practice conventionally. What fails is the demand that knowledge be grounded in something intrinsically self-validating.

9. Mind-body: neither dualism nor reductive physicalism

Nāgārjuna’s position is often misclassified because modern philosophy of mind tends to sort theories into dualism, physicalism, idealism, and neutral monism. Madhyamaka does not fit these categories neatly.

It is not dualism. There is no independent mental substance over against material substance. It is not reductive physicalism. The physical body, too, is empty and dependently designated. It is not idealism. Consciousness is not the ultimate ground of matter. It is not eliminativism. Mental events, persons, and suffering are conventionally real and ethically significant.

Buddhist analysis begins from psycho-physical aggregates, only one of which is material form, while the others include sensation, apperception, volitional formations, and consciousness. The Buddhist analysis does not imply physicalism in the modern sense, because materiality is analyzed phenomenologically and causally rather than as an ultimate substance.

A Nāgārjunian mind-body theory would therefore say: body and mind are dependently co-arisen explanatory categories. Bodily events condition mental events; mental events condition bodily action; both are conventionally describable; neither has intrinsic nature. The mind-body problem arises when “mind” and “body” are treated as independently intelligible substances or domains whose relation must then be explained. Nāgārjuna dissolves that starting point.

This is not the same as saying the hard problem of consciousness is a pseudo-problem in a dismissive sense. It is saying that the hard problem is generated by reifying both sides: “physical reality” as intrinsically objective and “phenomenal consciousness” as intrinsically subjective. If both are empty, then the demand for an intrinsic bridge between them is itself suspect.

10. Agency, karma, and responsibility without a substantial agent

Nāgārjuna must preserve agency because Buddhist practice depends on action, ethical causality, and liberation. Yet he cannot accept an intrinsically existing agent. The agent, action, instrument, and result are mutually dependent. If one treats them as intrinsically real, causal and ethical processes become unintelligible; if one treats them as non-existent, practice and responsibility collapse.

In MMK 24, Nāgārjuna argues that if things existed from their own nature, they would not depend on causes and conditions; cause and effect, agents, tools, acts, production, cessation, and results would be undermined.

This yields a sophisticated account of responsibility. Responsibility does not require an unchanging soul. It requires causal continuity, intentional patterns, memory, affective conditioning, social recognition, and practical accountability. A person is not an intrinsic agent, but agency is conventionally real as a pattern in the dependently arisen stream.

This has contemporary relevance. Nāgārjuna avoids both libertarian metaphysics, which posits a self-causing self, and hard determinist eliminativism, which treats agency as mere illusion. Agency is neither ultimately real nor simply unreal. It is conventionally real and ultimately empty.

11. Suffering as a cognitive-affective error

Nāgārjuna’s philosophy of mind is therapeutic. Its target is not only false belief but the cognitive-affective structure of suffering. We suffer because we grasp: at self, mine, object, enemy, permanence, purity, control, metaphysical certainty. The mind projects svabhāva and then becomes emotionally bound to its projection.

In MMK 18, Nāgārjuna connects the cessation of “I” and “mine” grasping with the cessation of clinging, and the cessation of clinging with the cessation of birth.

This is not merely a metaphysical thesis. It is a psychology of affective construction. The mind does not neutrally represent a world and then add emotions. Rather, affect, valuation, self-reference, and conceptual proliferation are intertwined. “This is mine,” “this threatens me,” “this proves who I am,” “this must remain,” “this must be destroyed”: these are not accidental overlays but central structures of afflicted cognition.

The realization of emptiness is therefore a transformation in appearance, not only assent to a proposition. The Stanford entry emphasizes that intellectual understanding of emptiness is not the same as realization; realization changes how the world appears, much as understanding an optical illusion does not by itself stop the illusion from appearing.

This is a point of great importance for philosophy of mind. Nāgārjuna distinguishes propositional belief from perceptual-affective transformation. One can believe that the self is empty and still experience the world as centered on a solid “me.” The philosophy of mind implicit here includes a layered model: conceptual endorsement, perceptual appearance, affective habit, and embodied response can come apart.

12. Nāgārjuna’s method: philosophy as de-reification

Nāgārjuna does not offer a single master argument for emptiness. His method proceeds case by case: causation, motion, aggregates, perception, self, time, agency, truth, nirvāṇa. The reason is internal to the view: if nothing has intrinsic nature, there cannot be one context-independent argument that intrinsically establishes emptiness for all audiences and all conceptual frameworks.

This method is often called prasaṅga, reductio or consequence reasoning, especially in later Tibetan classifications. Rather than beginning by asserting a rival metaphysical thesis, Nāgārjuna shows that the opponent’s essentialist assumptions produce contradictions. If causation has intrinsic nature, causation becomes impossible. If the self has intrinsic nature, experience becomes unintelligible. If language mirrors intrinsic structure, reference cannot be grounded. If epistemic instruments are self-established, they cannot explain their relation to objects.

This makes Nāgārjuna’s philosophy of mind diagnostic rather than constructive in the usual sense. He does not ask, “What is the ultimate nature of mind?” He asks, “What goes wrong when we assume mind must have an ultimate nature?”

A reconstructed Nāgārjunian philosophy of mind

The following is the systematic doctrine that can be extracted.

First, mental life is processual. Mind is not a thing but an interdependent stream of events: sensation, perception, feeling, intention, attention, memory, conceptualization, affect, and consciousness. These processes are not reducible to a single substance or owner.

Second, the self is a dependent designation. The person is neither identical with the aggregates nor different from them. The self exists conventionally as a pattern of psycho-physical continuity, social designation, and practical agency, but not ultimately as an independent entity.

Third, consciousness is conditioned. Consciousness does not function as a metaphysical ground. It arises dependently with sense faculty, object, attention, and conditions. It cannot establish itself as intrinsically real.

Fourth, perception is relational. There is no intrinsically existing seer, seeing, or seen. Perception is a co-dependent event, not a metaphysical bridge between two pre-formed substances.

Fifth, intentionality is empty. Mental states are about objects only within relational, conceptual, causal, and practical contexts. Object-directedness is real conventionally but not intrinsically.

Sixth, mental content is dependently designated. Concepts and words do not mirror a ready-made world with intrinsic joints. They function through convention, use, pragmatic success, and shared forms of life.

Seventh, affective suffering arises from reification. The mind’s core pathology is not cognition as such but grasping at intrinsically real self, object, value, threat, possession, and identity.

Eighth, insight is transformative. Understanding emptiness as a proposition is not enough. The goal is a shift in how phenomena appear and how the cognitive-affective system responds.

Ninth, epistemic authority is contextual. Perception, inference, introspection, and testimony can work, but none is self-grounding. Means of knowledge and objects of knowledge are mutually established.

Tenth, agency is conventionally real. Ethical action does not require an intrinsic agent. It requires dependently arisen patterns of intention, action, consequence, and responsibility.

Eleventh, mind and body are co-dependent. Nāgārjuna avoids both dualism and reductive physicalism. Body and mind are useful conventional categories, but neither is metaphysically ultimate.

Twelfth, emptiness is not an ontology of nothingness. It is the absence of intrinsic nature, and that absence is precisely what allows causal, mental, ethical, and liberative processes to function. Nāgārjuna explicitly argues in MMK 24 that if things were not empty, arising, ceasing, suffering, and liberation would become impossible.

Comparison with Western philosophy of mind

Plato, Aristotle, and ancient substance psychology

Relative to Plato and much of the later Western tradition, Nāgārjuna rejects the idea that the person is grounded in an enduring soul-substance. Relative to Aristotle, he also resists the idea that living beings have intrinsic forms or essences that explain their identity and activity. Aristotle’s hylomorphism is less dualistic than Plato’s or Descartes’s, but it still explains beings through form, nature, and teleological organization. Nāgārjuna’s critique would ask whether “form,” “nature,” and “organism” can be established independently of conceptual and causal dependence.

The deeper contrast is that classical Greek philosophy often seeks stable intelligibility: form, substance, essence, or rational order. Nāgārjuna treats that desire for stable intelligibility as itself a source of reification. He does not deny intelligibility; he denies intrinsic intelligibility.

Pyrrhonian skepticism

Nāgārjuna is often compared to Pyrrhonian skeptics because both use arguments to undermine dogmatic positions and both see philosophical release as therapeutic. The convergence is real: both traditions use reason against metaphysical fixation.

But Nāgārjuna is not merely a skeptic in the Western sense. He has a positive soteriological framework: dependent origination, emptiness, the cessation of grasping, and liberation. He also accepts conventional truth and practical efficacy. The goal is not simply suspension of judgment but the end of reifying cognition.

Descartes and the Cartesian legacy

Descartes makes mind-body dualism a central starting point for modern philosophy. In the standard Cartesian picture, mind and body are distinct substances: mind is characterized by thought, body by extension, and substance is something capable of existing by itself. This dualist framework became a standard starting point for later mind-body debates.

Nāgārjuna is almost the perfect anti-Cartesian. The Cartesian search for certainty begins with the thinking subject. Nāgārjuna’s analysis denies that the thinking subject is self-established. The occurrence of thought does not prove an intrinsically existing thinker. “Thinking occurs” may be conventionally acceptable; “therefore there is a self-grounding mental substance” is precisely the kind of inference Madhyamaka rejects.

Descartes also takes introspective access to mind as epistemically privileged. Nāgārjuna’s pramāṇa critique undermines that privilege. No cognition, including introspection, is intrinsically self-validating. It functions only within a network of conditions, objects, practices, and correction.

Hume and bundle theory

Hume is the closest major Western analogue. Contemporary discussions of personal identity list Hume’s view as the claim that we are collections or bundles of mental states or events.

The convergence is substantial: both reject a persisting ego-substance and replace it with changing experiential processes. The Stanford entry on dualism even notes that Hume’s replacement of the Cartesian mind by a bundle of fleeting perceptions is reminiscent of, and possibly influenced by, earlier Buddhist theories.

The differences are just as important. Hume’s bundle theory is primarily empiricist and psychological. Nāgārjuna’s account is ontological, epistemological, semantic, ethical, and soteriological. More importantly, Hume often treats perceptions as the basic contents of the bundle, whereas Nāgārjuna would deny that the bundle-elements have intrinsic nature. Nāgārjuna is not only anti-ego; he is anti-foundational with respect to the constituents that might replace the ego.

Kant: construction, synthesis, and the limits of self-knowledge

Kant is another deep point of comparison. Kant treats mind as an active complex of functions: cognition requires both sensory input and conceptual synthesis, and unity of consciousness is central to cognition. The SEP notes that Kant’s model anticipates functionalist themes and influenced later cognitive science.

Nāgārjuna and Kant converge in rejecting naive realism. The mind is not a passive mirror of a ready-made world. Both also deny that we know the self as it is in itself. Kant distinguishes empirical self-consciousness from transcendental apperception and holds that self-consciousness does not yield knowledge of the self as it is in itself.

But Kant retains transcendental structures as necessary conditions of experience. Nāgārjuna is more radical: any such structure, if treated as intrinsically grounding experience, would also be empty. Kant asks, “What must the mind contribute for experience to be possible?” Nāgārjuna asks, “What happens when we reify either mind, contribution, experience, or possibility?”

Wittgenstein, language, and the private mental object

Nāgārjuna converges strongly with later Wittgenstein on the critique of private meaning. Wittgenstein’s private language argument targets the idea that words could refer to what only the speaker can know, such as purely private immediate sensations.

Nāgārjuna’s analysis is not the same argument, but it shares a suspicion of intrinsically private mental reference. If meanings are dependently designated, then the meaning of a sensation-word cannot be fixed by a private essence grasped inwardly. Pain-language functions through embodied expression, social training, practical use, memory, correction, and convention. This does not mean pain is unreal. It means pain is not an intrinsically self-interpreting private object.

Sellars and the Myth of the Given

Sellars’s critique of the “Myth of the Given” attacks the idea that there is a non-conceptual, self-authenticating given that can serve as the foundation of knowledge. The SEP summarizes Sellars as needing a positive account of non-inferential knowledge after rejecting the Given.

Nāgārjuna’s pramāṇa critique is structurally similar. Nothing is simply given with intrinsic epistemic authority. Sensory experience can function as a source of knowledge conventionally, but it is not self-grounding. Its epistemic status depends on relations to objects, practices, successful action, coherence, correction, and context.

The difference is that Sellars is working within post-Kantian analytic epistemology, whereas Nāgārjuna embeds the critique in a path of liberation from grasping. For Nāgārjuna, the Myth of the Given is not only epistemologically mistaken; it is existentially binding.

Pragmatism

Pragmatism and Madhyamaka converge in their suspicion of metaphysical absolutes and their attention to practices of inquiry and assertion. Pragmatic theories of truth focus on the relation between truth and epistemic practices such as inquiry and assertion, and classical pragmatism used the pragmatic maxim to clarify concepts by tracing practical consequences.

Nāgārjuna similarly treats conventional truth as embedded in use, practice, and successful functioning rather than correspondence to intrinsic reality. But he is not simply a pragmatist. Conventional success is not the final goal; it is part of a soteriological path. A belief can be conventionally useful and still reinforce grasping. Madhyamaka asks not only “What works?” but “Does this mode of cognition reify?”

Phenomenology and embodied cognition

Nāgārjuna has affinities with phenomenology because both resist the simple objectivist picture of mind as a detached spectator representing a ready-made world. Merleau-Ponty, for example, begins from perception and embodiment, seeking an alternative to intellectualism or idealism on one side and empiricism or realism on the other, while criticizing the presupposition of a ready-made world.

Contemporary embodied cognition also challenges the idea that cognition is just computation in the brain; it emphasizes the role of the body and the body’s interaction with the environment in cognition.

Nāgārjuna converges with these views in rejecting isolated mind-internalism. But again, he is more radical. He would not merely replace brain-centered cognition with body-world cognition. Body, world, environment, and interaction are themselves empty. The embodied mind is not a new foundation; it is a more adequate conventional description of dependent arising.

Physicalism, functionalism, and contemporary analytic philosophy

Contemporary physicalism says, broadly, that mind is fundamentally physical. Functionalism says mental states are individuated by functional roles. Nāgārjuna would treat both as potentially useful conventional research programs and potentially misleading metaphysical doctrines.

A Madhyamaka physicalism would say: mental phenomena depend on bodily and neural conditions. But if physicalism means that the physical is an intrinsic ultimate reality, Nāgārjuna rejects it. A Madhyamaka functionalism would say: mental states are intelligible through roles in causal-practical networks. But if functional roles are treated as intrinsic essences, Nāgārjuna rejects that too.

Nāgārjuna’s view is not anti-scientific. It is anti-reification. Neuroscience, psychology, cognitive science, phenomenology, and social theory may all yield valid conventional accounts. None yields the intrinsic nature of mind.

Idealism and Yogācāra

Nāgārjuna must also be distinguished from Buddhist idealism. Yogācāra philosophers such as Dignāga and Dharmakīrti developed arguments that the object of cognition cannot be an external entity and that what we perceive is an appearance within cognition.

Madhyamaka resists making consciousness the final ground. From a Madhyamaka standpoint, idealism avoids reifying external matter but risks reifying mind. Nāgārjuna’s position is not “all is mind” but “mind and matter are empty.” This is why Nāgārjuna is especially useful for contemporary philosophy of mind: he offers a way out of both materialist and idealist foundationalism.

How Nāgārjuna reframes major philosophy-of-mind problems

The hard problem of consciousness

The hard problem asks why and how physical processes give rise to subjective experience. Nāgārjuna would diagnose this as arising from a dual reification: “the physical” is treated as intrinsically objective, and “experience” is treated as intrinsically subjective. Then philosophy searches for a bridge.

Madhyamaka does not solve the hard problem by reducing consciousness to matter or matter to consciousness. It dissolves the assumption that either side has intrinsic nature. Subjective experience exists conventionally; physical processes exist conventionally; their relation is dependently describable. The demand for an ultimate metaphysical bridge presupposes what emptiness denies.

The self-model and narrative identity

Nāgārjuna anticipates contemporary views that the self is constructed, narrative, embodied, and socially mediated. But he adds that construction does not mean sheer illusion. The self is a dependent designation that can be accurate enough for ethics and practice while still being empty.

The dangerous self-model is not the conventional “I” used to navigate life. It is the reified “I” experienced as owner, controller, essence, and metaphysical center. The goal is not to abolish conventional self-reference but to remove grasping.

Qualia and private experience

Nāgārjuna would not deny that there is pain, color-experience, taste, or emotion. But he would deny that qualia are intrinsic private atoms of consciousness. The felt character of experience is dependently arisen: body, attention, contrast, memory, language, affect, and practical orientation shape it. Qualitative experience is real conventionally but empty ultimately.

Mental causation

If mind and body are treated as intrinsically distinct, mental causation becomes mysterious. If mind is reduced away, agency becomes difficult to preserve. Nāgārjuna’s approach avoids both: mental events can causally condition bodily action conventionally because both are dependently arisen. There is no need for an intrinsic mental substance to push an intrinsic physical substance.

AI and artificial minds

Nāgārjuna does not directly address artificial intelligence, of course. But a Madhyamaka framework would caution against asking whether an AI has an intrinsic mind-essence. The better questions are relational and conventional: What capacities arise? What forms of responsiveness, self-modeling, suffering, social embeddedness, learning, agency, and ethical salience are present? Madhyamaka would resist both “only biological beings have real mind by essence” and “functional equivalence automatically gives intrinsic consciousness.” Both are essentialist shortcuts.

Misreadings to avoid

First, emptiness is not nihilism. Nāgārjuna’s opponents claim that if all is empty, causation, the four noble truths, and practice collapse. Nāgārjuna replies that this misunderstands emptiness; in fact, emptiness is what allows dependent arising, change, suffering, and liberation to make sense.

Second, emptiness is not idealism. Nāgārjuna does not say that everything is mental. Consciousness is empty too.

Third, no-self is not the denial of persons in every sense. Persons exist conventionally; they lack intrinsic nature. This distinction is essential for ethics.

Fourth, Madhyamaka is not simple skepticism. It does not deny all knowledge. It denies self-grounding knowledge.

Fifth, realization is not merely intellectual. Madhyamaka aims at a transformation of appearance, affect, and grasping.

Sixth, conventional truth is not “mere falsehood.” Without convention, the ultimate cannot be taught. Convention is indispensable, even though it is empty.

Final synthesis

Nāgārjuna’s philosophy of mind can be stated as follows:

Mental life is a dependently arisen, conventionally intelligible, causally efficacious, ethically significant, and ultimately empty stream of psycho-physical processes. The self is not an owner of this stream but a dependent designation upon it. Consciousness is not an ultimate witness but a conditioned event. Perception is not a relation between intrinsically real subject and object but a co-dependent structure. Concepts and language do not mirror intrinsic reality but organize experience through convention and use. Suffering arises when the mind reifies what is empty, especially self and object. Liberation requires not the destruction of mind but the de-reification of experience.

Historically, this places Nāgārjuna among the most radical anti-foundational philosophers of mind. Compared with Western traditions, he is more anti-substantialist than Aristotle, more anti-Cartesian than Hume, more anti-foundational than Kant, more soteriological than Wittgenstein, more ontologically radical than pragmatism, and more non-reductive than physicalism. His deepest contribution is not a theory of consciousness as a thing, but a critique of the very impulse to find the thing that consciousness really is.

For further rigorous study, the most accessible modern philosophical translations of the MMK include Jay Garfield’s The Fundamental Wisdom of the Middle Way and the Siderits–Katsura translation, both listed by the Stanford Encyclopedia as key English resources.

PreviousPatañjali NextVasubandhu

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
